metadata.title: Ingredients First: Pick 3 Sources with the Tier System
metadata.playlist: Shop for Ingredients — Collect Your Madison Data
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 3 brief — Shop for Ingredients: Collect Your Madison Data

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 1 of 7. Before you touch n8n, before you write a single line of workflow, you need to decide what you are collecting and where it comes from. That decision determines whether your project runs to the end — or gets blocked by a source that cuts you off after 50 requests. The tier system tells you which sources are safe to build on and which ones will burn your week. Two rules that run through everything: quality beats quantity — 50 clean records beats 1,000 messy ones. And collect once, reuse forever — run the workflow once, save the data, then work from the file. Today you walk out with a source plan: three Tier-1 sources mapped to your A2 problem. Let's go.

props.segment: Ingredients First: Pick 3 Sources with the Tier System
props.command: Pick 3 data sources for the A2 problem — Tier-1 only, mapped to the problem.
props.runningText: checking source reliability before you build anything…
output: Tier 1 (safe): public datasets, RSS feeds, YouTube/News APIs
Tier 3 (avoid): live scraping, LinkedIn API, Twitter free tier

beats.narration_text: Tier 1 is where you start. These are sources that let you pull data reliably, repeatedly, and for free. Public datasets: Kaggle, HuggingFace, Google Dataset Search, Data.gov, GitHub — search for your topic and you will usually find a dataset someone already cleaned. RSS feeds: nearly every news site and blog publishes one, no login required, no rate limit to worry about. API sources on Tier 1: YouTube Data API gives you ten thousand units per day free — more than enough for a student project. NewsAPI gives you one hundred free requests per day. Both require a free API key, not a paid account. These three source types — dataset, RSS, API — are the building blocks of your workflow. Pick one of each, and you cover three different kinds of real-world data.

props.artifactHeading: Tier 1 sources — safe to build on
artifactLines: Public datasets: Kaggle · HuggingFace · Google Dataset Search · Data.gov · GitHub
RSS feeds: any news site / blog — no login, no rate limit, free
YouTube Data API: 10,000 units/day free — requires a free API key
NewsAPI: 100 requests/day free — requires a free API key
Strategy: pick one dataset + one RSS feed + one API → three different data types.
props.sparkLine: One dataset. One RSS. One API.

beats.narration_text: Tier 2 is data you can use if you are careful about where you get it. The rule for Tier 2 is: pre-saved archives only, never live scraping. Kaggle hosts datasets that were exported from Twitter and Reddit by researchers before those platforms changed their API terms. Those datasets are already saved — they're static files you download once and work from. That is Tier 2. The reason it's Tier 2 and not Tier 1 is recency: a pre-saved archive may be months or years old. If your A2 problem requires current data, a Tier-2 archive may not serve it. If it needs historical trends, it may be exactly right. Check the dataset's date range before you commit to it.

artifactLines: Pre-saved Kaggle Twitter/Reddit archives: static files, download once, no API needed.
Check the date range — archives may be months or years old.
Good for: historical trends, NLP training sets, sentiment datasets.
Not good for: anything that needs data from this week or this month.
Rule: pre-saved is fine. Live scraping from Tier-2 platforms is Tier 3.
props.sparkLine: Download the archive. Don't scrape live.

beats.narration_text: Tier 3 is the avoid list — and the reason is not a policy, it's a practical reality. LinkedIn API requires a partner application that takes weeks and is almost never approved for student projects. Twitter and X's free tier cuts you off after 1,500 reads per month and has changed its terms several times in the past two years. Reddit's live API throttles and blocks scrapers. Business verification APIs like Clearbit charge per request. These sources are not Tier 3 because the data is bad — some of it is excellent. They are Tier 3 because they will block a student project before the deadline. The lesson is this: you learned in Assignment 2 that these sources gate you. The tier system encodes that experience. Do not repeat it.

props.artifactHeading: Tier 3 sources — will block your project
artifactLines: LinkedIn API: partner approval required — weeks of waiting, almost never approved for students.
Twitter/X free tier: 1,500 read/month cap; terms change; will cut you off mid-project.
Reddit live scraping: throttles and blocks scrapers; pre-saved archives (Tier 2) are the alternative.
Business-verification APIs (Clearbit, etc.): charged per request — no free student tier.
Rule: if getting blocked derails your deadline, it's Tier 3.
props.sparkLine: The source that blocks you isn't free.

beats.narration_text: Searching Kaggle is simple. Go to kaggle.com and type your topic plus the word 'dataset' in the search bar. Filter by file type if you need CSV. Sort by votes to see what the community has found useful. Look for datasets with at least a hundred rows and at least three columns that map to your A2 problem. Finding RSS feeds is also simple. Search for your topic plus 'RSS feed' in a browser. Most news sites have a feed URL at slash rss or slash feed. Paste the URL into a reader like Feedly to confirm it is active. If you cannot find a feed, the site's homepage will often link to one in the page source. For APIs: YouTube Data API and NewsAPI both have free tiers and their documentation takes about thirty minutes to read. Get the key before you start building — key provisioning can take a few minutes, and you do not want to be waiting on it while your workflow is half-wired.

artifactLines: Kaggle: search '[topic] dataset' → filter by CSV → sort by votes → check column count vs. your problem.
RSS: search '[topic] RSS feed' → paste URL into Feedly to confirm it is active and recent.
APIs: get your free YouTube Data API or NewsAPI key before you start building — takes minutes.
Target: 3 sources with fields that address the A2 problem. No filler sources.
props.sparkLine: Keys before workflow. Fields before records.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Ingredients First. Two rules run through the whole assignment: quality beats quantity — 50 clean records beats 1,000 messy ones — and collect once, reuse forever — run the workflow once, save the file, then work from the file. Your source plan has three Tier-1 sources, each mapped to the A2 problem, each with a specific field list. Tier 1 is the working set: public datasets, RSS feeds, YouTube Data API, NewsAPI. Tier 2 is pre-saved archives only. Tier 3 is the sources that blocked the A2 work — LinkedIn, live Twitter, live Reddit, business APIs — avoid them. Before Episode 2, get your YouTube Data API or NewsAPI key if you plan to use one. Key provisioning takes minutes; do not start wiring nodes without it.

artifactLines: Rule 1 — Quality over quantity: 50 clean records beats 1,000 messy ones.
Rule 2 — Collect once, reuse forever: run once, save the file, work from the file.
Tier 1: public datasets · RSS feeds · YouTube Data API (10k/day) · NewsAPI (100/day).
Tier 2: pre-saved archives only — check the date range before committing.
Tier 3 (avoid): LinkedIn API · live Twitter/X · live Reddit scraping · paid business APIs.
Before E2: get your API key if you plan to use one — key provisioning takes minutes.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this prompt into Claude — fill in the bracketed fields with your actual A2 problem statement before you run it. The prompt reads: I am building a Madison agent for this problem: [paste your A2 problem statement in one or two sentences]. Suggest three Tier-1 data sources — a public dataset, an RSS feed, and a free API — that would give me records relevant to this problem. For each source, name the specific fields I should save and explain why each field connects to the problem. Then list two Tier-3 sources I might be tempted to use and explain exactly why each one would block a student project. That prompt does two things: it gives you your source plan, and it forces you to articulate why you are not using the blocked sources. The Tier-3 explanation is what you write in the documentation for Episode 6.

props.segment: Ingredients First: Pick 3 Sources with the Tier System
props.command: I am building a Madison agent for this problem: [paste your A2 problem statement]. Suggest 3 Tier-1 sources — a public dataset, an RSS feed, and a free API. For each: name the specific fields to save and why each connects to the problem. Then name 2 Tier-3 sources I might try and explain exactly why each blocks a student project.
props.runningText: fill in your A2 problem before running — the source plan only works with a real problem…
